sentence_de: Meine Schwester lebt in Düsseldorf
translation_en: My sister lives in Düsseldorf
question_en: What does the verb tell you about the subject?
explanation_en: The verb 'lebt' is the third-person singular of 'leben'. The '-t' ending is like a fingerprint; whenever you see it, you know the subject is one person: er, sie, or es
explanation_tr: Lebt' fiili, 'leben' fiilinin üçüncü tekil şahıs hâlidir. '-t' takısı bir parmak izi gibidir; gördüğünüzde öznenin tek bir kişi olduğunu anlarsınız: er, sie veya es

sentence_de: In Deutschland leben etwa 3 Millionen Türken
translation_en: In Germany, approximately 3 million Turks live
question_en: Which one is the subject of the sentence?
explanation_en: The subject does the action, not always at the beginning. 'leben' (live) agrees with '3 Millionen Türken' (they)
explanation_tr: Eylemi yapan öznedir, cümlenin başında olmak zorunda değildir

sentence_de: Ich habe keine Schmerzen
translation_en: I have no pain
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Schmerzen"?
explanation_en: Keine' is used to negate a noun like 'Schmerzen'. Whenever you want to say 'no' to a thing, reach for 'kein', whenever you want to say 'not' to an action, reach for 'nicht'
explanation_tr: Schmerzen' bir isim olduğu için 'keine' ile olumsuz yapılır. Bir şeye 'yok' demek istediğinizde 'kein', bir eyleme 'değil' demek istediğinizde 'nicht' kullanın

sentence_de: Er hat blonde Haare
translation_en: He has blonde hair
question_en: What does the verb tell you about the subject?
explanation_en: The verb changes for each subject: 'ich habe', 'du hast', 'er/sie/es hat'
explanation_tr: Fiil her şahsa göre değişir: 'ich habe', 'du hast', 'er/sie/es hat'

sentence_de: Wer ist diese Frau?
translation_en: Who is this woman?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Frau"?
explanation_en: Frau' is a feminine word, so it always uses the article 'die' or 'diese'. Every noun is born with a gender; learning the article together with the word is the fastest way to feel it naturally
explanation_tr: Frau' dişil olduğundan her zaman 'die' veya 'diese' ile kullanılır. Her ismin bir cinsiyeti vardır; kelimeyi artikeliyle birlikte öğrenmek en doğru yoldur

sentence_de: Ich war beim Arzt
translation_en: I was at the doctor
question_en: What does the verb tell you about the time?
explanation_en: In everyday speech, 'sein' and 'haben' are usually used in the simple past as 'war' and 'hatte'
explanation_tr: Günlük konuşmada 'sein' ve 'haben' fiilleri geçmiş zamandan bahsederken genellikle 'war' ve 'hatte' şeklinde kullanılır

sentence_de: Sie ist kein netter Mensch
translation_en: She is not a nice person
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Mensch"?
explanation_en: Mensch' is always a masculine noun regardless of biological sex; therefore, the article and adjective must match its grammatical gender even when describing a woman. Grammar gender and real gender are two different worlds
explanation_tr: ‘Mensch’ biyolojik cinsiyetten bağımsız olarak her zaman erildir; bu nedenle, bir kadını tanımlarken bile ismin dilbilgisel cinsiyetine uyulmalıdır. Almancada gramer cinsiyeti ile gerçek cinsiyet iki ayrı dünyadır

sentence_de: Ich habe keine Kopfschmerzen mehr
translation_en: I no longer have a headache
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'keine'?
explanation_en: Kopfschmerzen' is plural so 'kein' takes '-e' to become 'keine'. To negate a verb use 'nicht' instead
explanation_tr: Kopfschmerzen' çoğul olduğu için 'kein' sonuna '-e' alır. Fiilleri olumsuz yapmak için 'nicht' kullanılır

sentence_de: Menschen müssen eine Maske tragen
translation_en: People must wear a mask
question_en: Is the subject one person or many?
question_tr: Özne tekil kişi mi yoksa birden fazla mı?
explanation_en: Menschen' is the plural form of 'Mensch' and the verb 'müssen' is conjugated for a plural subject
explanation_tr: Menschen' kelimesi çoğul hâldedir ve fiil 'müssen' de bu çoğul özneye göre çekimlenmiştir

sentence_de: Ich habe starke Kopfschmerzen
translation_en: I have a severe headache
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Kopfschmerzen"?
explanation_en: For compound nouns like 'Kopfschmerzen', the last word 'Schmerzen' decides the gender. 'Schmerzen' makes the word plural. In compound nouns, always look at the last piece, it is the boss
explanation_tr: Kopfschmerzen' gibi bileşik isimlerde, son kelime 'Schmerzen' cinsiyeti belirler. 'Schmerzen' çoğul olduğu için kelime çoğuldur. Bileşik isimlerde her zaman son parçaya bakın, patron odur

sentence_de: Mein Bruder ist sehr groß
translation_en: My brother is very tall
question_en: What case is used in the sentence?
explanation_en: With the verb “ist” (to be), the subject and what follows it both stay in the nominative
explanation_tr: “Ist” fiili kullanıldığında, özne ve onu takip eden kısım nominativ olur

sentence_de: Du sollst viel Wasser trinken
translation_en: You should drink plenty of water
question_en: What is happening with the verbs in this sentence?
explanation_en: The modal verb 'sollst' is in position 2. The main verb 'trinken' goes to the end. This is the 'modal verb' pattern
explanation_tr: sollst' modal fiili 2. sıradadır. 'trinken' asıl fiili mastar hâlinde sona gider. Bu, 'modal fiil' kalıbıdır

sentence_de: Ich gehe heute zum Arzt
translation_en: I'm going to the doctor today
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Arzt"?
explanation_en: Arzt' is a masculine noun. The article 'der' becomes 'dem' after 'zu'
explanation_tr: Arzt' eril bir isimdir. 'der' artikeli 'zu' edatından sonra 'dem' olur

sentence_de: Die Kinder waschen ihre Hände
translation_en: The children wash their hands
question_en: What does 'die' tell you about the subject?
explanation_en: The subject 'die Kinder' uses 'die' because it is a plural noun. All plural nouns take 'die' as their article. 'die' could also be feminine singular, but the verb 'waschen' confirms it is plural here
explanation_tr: Özne 'die Kinder' çoğul bir isim olduğu için 'die' artikeli kullanılır. Tüm çoğul isimler artikel olarak 'die' alır. 'die' aynı zamanda dişil tekil de olabilir, ancak 'waschen' fiili burada çoğul olduğunu doğrular

sentence_de: Er sieht heute leider nicht gut aus
translation_en: He doesn't look well today, unfortunately
question_en: What is happening with the verb 'aussehen' here?
explanation_en: The verb 'aussehen' splits: 'sieht' stays in second position, and the prefix 'aus' goes to the end of the sentence. The prefix always waits patiently at the end, like a bookmark
explanation_tr: aussehen' fiili ikiye ayrılır: 'sieht' ikinci pozisyonda kalır, 'aus' öneki cümlenin sonuna gider. Önek her zaman sabırla sonda bekler, bir ayraç gibi

sentence_de: Danke für deine Hilfe
translation_en: Thank you for your help
question_en: What is the case of the sentence?
explanation_en: The word 'für' always uses the Akkusative case for the noun that follows it
explanation_tr: für' kelimesi kendisinden sonraki ismi her zaman Belirtme hâli (Akuzatif) yapar

sentence_de: Sprechen Sie Englisch?
translation_en: Do you speak English?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'Sie'?
question_tr: Bu resmi mi yoksa samimi 'sen/siz' mi?
explanation_en: Sie' is the formal 'you' because 'sprechen' is conjugated for it. Capital 'Sie' is your clue; lowercase 'sie' means 'she' or 'they', capital 'Sie' always means formal 'you'
explanation_tr: Buradaki 'Sie' resmidir çünkü 'sprechen' fiili buna göre çekimlenmiştir. Büyük harf 'Sie' ipucunuzdur; küçük harf 'sie' 'o' veya 'onlar' demektir, büyük harf 'Sie' her zaman resmi 'siz' demektir

sentence_de: Ich verstehe dich
translation_en: I understand you
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'dich'?
explanation_en: verstehen' needs a direct object (whom?), so 'du' becomes 'dich' in the accusative case. Every pronoun has two faces; one for doing (du), one for receiving (dich)
explanation_tr: verstehen' fiili bir nesne (kimi?) gerektirir. Bu yüzden 'du' zamiri akuzatif hâlde 'dich' olur. Her zamirin iki yüzü vardır; yapan (du) ve etkilenen (dich)

sentence_de: Ich chatte mit meinen Freunden
translation_en: I am chatting with my friends
question_en: Which statement about “mit” is correct?
explanation_en: The word 'mit' shows who you are with or what you are doing something with, so it always uses the dative case
explanation_tr: mit' kiminle veya neyle birlikte olduğunu gösterir, bu yüzden hep Dativ gerektirir

sentence_de: Wollen Sie ein Wörterbuch?
translation_en: Do you want a dictionary?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Wörterbuch"?
explanation_en: When two words combine to form a new noun, its article is determined by the last word
explanation_tr: İki kelime birleştiğinde, oluşan kelimenin artikeli sondaki kelimeye göre belirlenir

sentence_de: Kannst du mir helfen?
translation_en: Can you help me?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'mir'?
explanation_en: helfen' always takes a dative object (to whom?), so 'ich' becomes 'mir' in the dative case
explanation_tr: helfen' fiili her zaman dativ nesne gerektirir (kime?). Bu yüzden 'ich' zamiri dativ hâlde 'mir' olur

sentence_de: Können Sie das noch einmal wiederholen?
translation_en: Could you repeat that?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The modal verb 'können' changes its form and stays at the beginning. The main verb 'wiederholen' stays in its original form at the end of the sentence
explanation_tr: können' bir modal fiildir, özneye göre çekilir ve başta durur. Ana fiil 'wiederholen' ise cümlenin sonunda, yalın hâlinde kalır

sentence_de: Er ruft seine Mutter an
translation_en: He calls his mother
question_en: Does this verb stay together or split apart?
question_tr: Buradaki fiil bir arada mı kalır yoksa ayrılır mı?
explanation_en: The verb 'anrufen' splits: 'ruft' stays with 'Er', and 'an' goes to the end of the sentence. Once you feel this split, you will start noticing it everywhere; 'an-', 'auf-', 'ein-', 'aus-', 'mit-', 'vor-' all do the same
explanation_tr: anrufen' fiili ayrılabilir: 'ruft' özne ile kalır ve 'an' cümlenin sonuna gider. Bu ayrılmayı hissettikten sonra her yerde fark etmeye başlarsınız; 'an-', 'auf-', 'ein-', 'aus-', 'mit-', 'vor-' hepsi aynısını yapar

sentence_de: Ich verstehe diesen Satz leider nicht
translation_en: I'm afraid I don't understand this sentence
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "nicht"?
explanation_en: nicht' negates the verb 'verstehe', meaning 'don't understand'. 'kein' is used when you negate a noun. Two tools, one job: 'nicht' for actions, 'kein' for things
explanation_tr: nicht' burada 'verstehe' fiilini olumsuz yapar. İsimleri olumsuz yapmak için 'kein' kullanılır. İki araç, bir iş: eylemler için 'nicht', şeyler için 'kein'

sentence_de: Wir sprechen im Kurs nur Deutsch
translation_en: We only speak German in the course
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Kurs"?
explanation_en: The word 'im' is a short form of 'in dem'. 'dem' shows that 'Kurs' is masculine and in the dative case
explanation_tr: im' kelimesi 'in dem'in kısa hâlidir. 'dem', 'Kurs'un eril ve dativ hâlinde olduğunu gösterir

sentence_de: Darf ich dich etwas fragen?
translation_en: May I ask you something?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: darf' is a modal verb that conjugates for 'ich'. It works with the main verb 'fragen' at the end of the sentence. The pattern is always the same; modal verb up front doing the grammar, main verb at the end carrying the meaning
explanation_tr: darf', 'ich' için çekimlenen bir modal fiildir. Cümlenin sonundaki 'fragen' yalın hâlde kullanılır. Kalıp her zaman aynıdır; modal fiil önde grameri yapar, asıl fiil sonda anlamı taşır

sentence_de: Bitte sprechen Sie langsam!
translation_en: Please speak slowly!
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The 'Sprechen Sie!' pattern is an imperative, used for polite commands
explanation_tr: Sprechen Sie!' kalıbı, kibar bir emir için kullanılan emir kipidir

sentence_de: Wie buchstabiert man Ihren Nachnamen?
translation_en: How do you spell your last name?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Nachnamen"?
explanation_en: Nachname' is a masculine noun because in compound words, the last word determines the gender
explanation_tr: Nachname' bir eril bir isimdir. Birleşik kelimelerde sondaki kelime ölçüt alınır

sentence_de: Ich antworte dir morgen auf deine Frage
translation_en: I'll answer your question tomorrow
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: antworten' adds standard endings like '-e' for 'ich' and does not change its main vowel, so it is regular conjugation
explanation_tr: antworten' fiili 'ich' için '-e' gibi standart ekler alır ve ünlü harfini değiştirmez; düzenli fiildir

sentence_de: Das ist meine Telefonnummer
translation_en: This is my phone number
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'meine'?
explanation_en: Nummer' is feminine (die Nummer), so the possessive 'mein' takes '-e' ending
explanation_tr: Nummer' dişil bir isimdir (die Nummer). Bu yüzden 'mein' iyelik zamiri '-e' takısı alır

sentence_de: Sie hat mir einen Brief geschickt
translation_en: She sent me a letter
question_en: When did this action happen?
explanation_en: The 'hat...ge-t' pattern is the Perfekt tense for past actions. 'mir' is dative (to me) and 'einen Brief' is accusative. When you see 'hat' at the start and 'ge-' at the end, something already happened
explanation_tr: hat...ge-t' kalıbı geçmişte olan eylemleri gösterir. 'mir' dativ (bana), 'einen Brief' akuzatifdir (mektubu). 'hat' başta ve 'ge-' sonda gördüğünüzde, bir şey çoktan olmuş demektir

sentence_de: Sie braucht ein neues Fahrrad
translation_en: She needs a new bicycle
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Fahrrad"?
explanation_en: Fahrrad' is in accusative. 'neu' takes the '-es' ending to identify the neuter gender of the noun, the article 'ein' remains in its base form. When 'ein' cannot show the gender, the adjective steps in and does the job
explanation_tr: Fahrrad' Akuzatif hâldedir. 'neu' sıfatı, ismin nötr (das) olduğunu göstermek için '-es' takısını alır, 'ein' artikeli ise yalın hâlinde kalır. 'ein' cinsiyeti gösteremediğinde, sıfat devreye girer ve bu işi üstlenir

sentence_de: Sind das Ihre Bücher?
translation_en: Are these your books?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Ihre"?
explanation_en: Because it is capitalized, it refers to formal 'Sie'. Because of 'Bücher' is plural and Nominative, 'Ihre' is also plural and Nominative
explanation_tr: Büyük harfle yazıldığı için resmî 'Siz' demektir. 'Bücher' çoğul ve Yalın hâl (Nominatif) olduğu için 'Ihre' de ona uygun çekimlenip sonuna '-e' almıştır

sentence_de: Was ist zum Essen?
translation_en: What's for dinner?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "zum"?
explanation_en: The preposition 'zu' requires the dative case. Since 'Essen' is a neuter noun (das Essen), 'das' becomes 'dem' in dative, so 'zu + dem' contracts to 'zum'. Whenever you see 'zum', you are actually seeing two words squeezed into one
explanation_tr: zu' edatı yönelme hâli (Dativ) gerektirir. 'Essen' nötr bir isim (das Essen) olduğu için Dativ hâlde 'dem' kullanılır ve 'zu + dem' birleşerek 'zum' olur. 'zum' gördüğünüzde aslında iki kelimenin tek kelimeye sıkıştığını görüyorsunuz

sentence_de: Wir stehen früh auf
translation_en: We get up early
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
question_tr: Cümlenin fiili için aşağıdakilerden hangisi doğrudur?
explanation_en: The verb 'aufstehen' consists of separable prefix 'auf-' and 'stehen'. In a normal sentence the prefix always travels to the end, no matter how long the sentence gets
explanation_tr: aufstehen' fiili, ayrılabilen önek 'auf-' ve 'stehen' fiilinden oluşur. Cümle ne kadar uzarsa uzasın ayrılabilen önek her zaman sona gider

sentence_de: Sie möchte ein Zimmer mit Balkon
translation_en: She would like a room with a balcony.
question_en: Is 'sie' one person or many here?
question_tr: Buradaki 'sie' tek kişi mi yoksa birden fazla mı?
explanation_en: Because the verb 'möchte' is conjugated for third-person singular (she), 'sie' is third-person singular. The verb is your clue; 'möchte' means one person, 'möchten' would mean many
explanation_tr: Fiil 'möchte' üçüncü tekil şahıs (dişil) için çekimlendiği için 'sie' dişil tekil şahıstır. Fiil ipucunuzdur; 'möchte' tek kişi demektir, 'möchten' olsaydı birden fazla kişi olurdu

sentence_de: Sie wohnen im ersten Stock
translation_en: They live on the first floor
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "im"?
explanation_en: Stock' is masculine. Because 'wohnen' indicates location, Dative case is used. 'im' is the contraction of 'in' and 'dem'
explanation_tr: Stock' erildir. 'wohnen' fiili yer bildirdiği için Dativ kullanılır. 'im', 'in' edatının 'dem' ile birleşmiş hâlidir

sentence_de: Er hat eine schöne Wohnung
translation_en: He has a nice apartment
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Wohnung"?
explanation_en: Nouns ending in '-ung' are feminine (die) — 'eine' and the adjective 'schöne' show this feminine gender
explanation_tr: -ung' ile biten isimler dişildir (die) — 'eine' ve 'schöne' sıfatındaki '-e' bu dişil cinsiyeti tanımlar

sentence_de: Ist das deine Wohnung?
translation_en: Is this your apartment?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "deine"?
explanation_en: Because 'Wohnung' is a feminine (die) noun, the possessive pronoun also becomes feminine and takes an -e ending
explanation_tr: Wohnung' dişil (die) bir isim olduğu için, iyelik zamiri de dişil hâle gelir ve sonuna -e takısı alır

sentence_de: Er kauft eine Lampe für das Schlafzimmer
translation_en: He buys a lamp for the bedroom.
translation_tr: Yatak odası için eski bir lamba alıyor
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Schlafzimmer"?
explanation_en: The word 'Schlafzimmer' is neuter because of 'Zimmer'; in compound words, the gender is determined by the last word
explanation_tr: ‘Schlafzimmer’ kelimesi ‘Zimmer’ nedeniyle nötrdür; birleşik kelimelerde cinsiyeti sondaki kelime belirler

sentence_de: Macht bitte die Heizung an
translation_en: Please turn on the heating.
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
option_2_en: It is in the request mood
explanation_en: It is a separable verb and in the imperative mood. The prefixes ‘an-’, ‘auf-’, ‘ab-’, ‘ein-’, ‘mit-’, and ‘zu-’ are separable prefixes
explanation_tr: Ayrılabilen bir fiildir ve emir kipindedir. 'an-', 'auf-', 'ab-', 'ein-', 'mit-', 'zu-' ayrılabilen ön eklerdir

sentence_de: Diese Maschine verbraucht Wasser
translation_en: This machine consumes water
question_en: Does this verb have a separable or inseparable prefix?
question_tr: Bu fiilin ön eki ayrılabilir mi yoksa ayrılamaz mı?
explanation_en: The prefix 'ver-' in 'verbraucht' is inseparable. Common inseparable prefixes are: be-, er-, ge-, ver-, zer-, ent-, emp-, miss-
explanation_tr: Fiilin ön eki 'ver-' ayrılamaz bir ön ektir. En yaygın ayrılamaz ön ekler: be-, er-, ge-, ver-, zer-, ent-, emp-, miss-

sentence_de: Wir möchten ein Auto kaufen
translation_en: We would like to buy a car
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Auto"?
explanation_en: Because 'kaufen' takes a direct object, 'Auto' is in accusative — 'ein' stays as 'ein' for neuter nouns in accusative case
explanation_tr: kaufen' fiili nesne aldığı için 'Auto' Akuzatifdir (Neyi) — Akuzatif hâlde nötr artikel (‘ein’) değişmez

sentence_de: Wo ist der Schlüssel?
translation_en: Where is the key?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Schlüssel"?
explanation_en: The article 'der' shows that 'Schlüssel' is masculine and in the nominative case
explanation_tr: der' artikeli 'Schlüssel'in eril ve yalın hâlde olduğunu gösterir

sentence_de: Ich bin nach Hause gekommen
translation_en: I came home
question_en: Which is correct for the verb of the sentence?
question_tr: Cümlenin fiili için aşağıdakilerden hangisi doğrudur?
explanation_en: Verbs showing movement to a new place, like 'kommen', use 'sein' (bin) for past actions. Most verbs use 'haben', but when there is a move from, 'sein' takes over
explanation_tr: Bir yerden bir yere gitme veya gelme gibi hareket fiilleri, geçmiş zamanda 'sein' (bin) ile oluşturur. Çoğu fiil 'haben' kullanır, ama hareket varsa 'sein' devreye girer

sentence_de: Das Haus hat drei Fenster
translation_en: The house has three windows
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
question_tr: Cümlenin fiili için aşağıdakilerden hangisi doğrudur?
explanation_en: Because 'Das Haus' is a singular subject, the third-person singular form 'hat' is used. For plural subjects, 'haben' is used
explanation_tr: Das Haus' tekil bir özne olduğu için 'haben' fiilinin üçüncü tekil şahıs çekimi olan 'hat' kullanılır. Çoğul özneler için 'haben' kullanılır

sentence_de: Wir wollen nach Hause gehen
translation_en: We want to go home
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "gehen"?
explanation_en: Modal verbs (wollen) are conjugated in the second position. The main verb (gehen) goes to the end in its infinitive form. Same as before; the modal does the grammar, the main verb carries the meaning at the end
explanation_tr: Modal fiiller (wollen) ikinci sırada özneye göre çekimlenir. Cümlenin asıl anlamını taşıyan ana fiil ise mastar hâlinde cümlenin sonuna gelir. Daha önceki gibi; modal fiil grameri yapar, asıl fiil anlamı sonda taşır

sentence_de: Sie hat eine Wohnung gemietet
translation_en: She rented an apartment
question_en: When did this action happen?
explanation_en: The past tense follows the 'hat...ge-' pattern; the verb 'gemietet' uses the auxiliary 'haben'
explanation_tr: Geçmiş zaman yapısı 'hat...ge-' kalıbıdır; 'gemietet' fiili 'haben' yardımcı fiiliyle kullanılır

sentence_de: Er hat den Schlüssel nicht gefunden
translation_en: He couldn't find the key.
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'hat gefunden' uses 'haben' with Partizip II, it is the form of the Perfekt (past) tense. 'gefunden' looks different from 'finden' because some verbs change shape completely in the past
explanation_tr: hat gefunden' fiili, 'haben' yardımcı fiili ve Partizip II ile kurulur, bu yapı Perfekt (geçmiş) zamanı gösterir. 'gefunden', 'finden'den farklı görünür çünkü bazı fiiller geçmiş zamanda şeklini tamamen değiştirir

sentence_de: Braucht er einen Schlüssel?
translation_en: Does he need a key?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Schlüssel"?
explanation_en: 'Schlüssel'' is a masculine noun and ''brauchen'' requires an object in the accusative case, so the indefinite article becomes ''einen''
explanation_tr: 'Schlüssel'' erildir ve ''brauchen'' fiili akuzatif hâlde bir nesne gerektirir, bu yüzden artikel ''einen'' olur

sentence_de: Er arbeitet als Koch
translation_en: He works as a cook
question_en: What does the verb 'arbeitet' tell you about the time?
question_tr: arbeitet' fiili zaman hakkında ne söylüyor?
explanation_en: The verb 'arbeiten' is conjugated for 'er' in the present tense (Präsens), which is third-person singular
explanation_tr: Fiil, 'er' öznesine göre şimdiki zamanda (Präsens) üçüncü tekil şahısa göre çekimlenmiştir

sentence_de: Du bist eine Schülerin
translation_en: You are a student
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Schülerin"?
explanation_en: Because 'Schülerin' ends with '-in', it is feminine, and it is in nominative case after 'sein'
explanation_tr: Schülerin' kelimesi '-in' takısı ile bittiği için dişildir ve 'sein' fiilinden sonra geldiği için Nominatif hâldedir

sentence_de: Ich arbeite bei einer Firma
translation_en: I work for a company
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "bei"?
explanation_en: bei' is a preposition that always makes the noun after it Dativ. 'Firma' is feminine noun, and it gets  'einer' (Dativ for 'eine')
explanation_tr: bei' edatı, kendisinden sonra gelen ismi her zaman Dativ yapar. 'Firma' dişildir ve 'ein' 'einer' olur

sentence_de: Was bist du von Beruf?
translation_en: What is your profession?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "von"?
explanation_en: Prepositions like 'von', 'mit', and 'aus' always make the noun that follows them Dativ
explanation_tr: von', 'mit' ve 'aus' gibi edatlar kendilerinden sonra gelen ismi her zaman dativ yapar

sentence_de: Mein Kollege kommt später
translation_en: My colleague will arrive later
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the subject?
explanation_en: The word 'mein' shows that 'Kollege' is a masculine noun
explanation_tr: Mein' kelimesi, 'Kollege' isminin eril olduğunu gösterir

sentence_de: Er schreibt dem Chef eine E-Mail
translation_en: He writes an email to the boss
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'schreibt' shows an action happening now. It does not use the 'hat... ge-t' pattern for past actions
explanation_tr: schreibt' eylemin şimdi olduğunu gösterir. Geçmiş zaman değil çünkü 'hat... ge-t' kalıbını kullanmıyor

sentence_de: Ich suche einen Job
translation_en: I'm looking for a job
question_en: What case is 'einen Job' in?
explanation_en: The verb 'suchen' requires a direct object in the accusative case. 'Job' is masculine, so 'ein' becomes 'einen'. Whenever a masculine noun is the thing being acted on, 'ein' takes an '-en'
explanation_tr: suchen' fiili akuzatif hâlde nesne gerektirir. 'Job' erildir, bu yüzden 'ein' 'einen' olur. Eril bir isim eylemin hedefi olduğunda, 'ein' bir '-en' takısı alır

sentence_de: Darf ich hier meinen Computer benutzen?
translation_en: May I use my computer here?
question_en: Which is the main verb in this sentence?
explanation_en: The modal verb 'Darf' is in position 2 and conjugated. The main verb 'benutzen' goes to the end in its infinitive form. The question to ask yourself: which verb carries the real action? That one is the main verb
explanation_tr: Modal fiil 'Darf' 2. sırada çekimli hâldedir. Asıl fiil 'benutzen' mastar hâlinde cümlenin sonuna gider. Soru şudur: hangi fiil gerçek eylemi taşıyor? O fiil asıl fiildir

sentence_de: Sie möchte heute nicht arbeiten
translation_en: She doesn't want to work today
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "nicht"?
explanation_en: This 'nicht' negates the verb 'arbeiten'. 'Nicht' negates actions, adjectives, and adverbs. 'Kein' negates nouns. If you can point at it, use 'kein'; if you can do it, use 'nicht'
explanation_tr: Buradaki 'nicht', 'arbeiten' fiilini olumsuz yapar. 'Nicht' fiilleri, sıfatları ve zarfları olumsuz yapar. 'Kein' ise isimleri olumsuz yapar. Gösterebiliyorsanız 'kein', yapabiliyorsanız 'nicht' kullanın

sentence_de: Haben Sie die Dokumente kopiert?
translation_en: Have you copied the documents?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the subject?
explanation_en: The capital 'S' in 'Sie' means 'you (formal)'. It uses the same verb form as 'wir' (we) or 'sie' (they). Watch the capital letter; it is the difference between 'she/they' and formal 'you'
explanation_tr: Büyük harfle yazılan 'Sie', resmi olarak 'siz' demektir. 'Wir' (biz) veya 'sie' (onlar) ile aynı fiil çekimini kullanır. Büyük harfe dikkat edin; 'o/onlar' ile resmi 'siz' arasındaki fark budur

sentence_de: Der Termin ist um zehn Uhr
translation_en: The appointment is at ten o'clock
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "um"?
option_2_en: Used for all the time expressions
explanation_en: um' is used for hours, 'am' for days, 'im' for months, seasons, and years
explanation_tr: um' saatlerde, 'am' günlerde, 'im' aylar, mevsimler, yıllarda kullanılır

sentence_de: Er schreibt einen Bericht
translation_en: He is writing a report
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Bericht"?
explanation_en: The 'einen' before 'Bericht' shows it is the direct object. For masculine words like 'Bericht', 'ein' changes to 'einen' in this pattern. This '-en' is the signature of masculine accusative
explanation_tr: Bericht' fiilin nesnesidir (neyi?). Bu kalıpta, eril kelimeler için 'ein' 'einen' hâline gelir. Bu '-en', eril akuzatifin imzasıdır

sentence_de: Bitte rufen Sie mich morgen an!
translation_en: Please call me tomorrow!
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: anrufen' is a separable verb. The prefix 'an' moves to the very end of the sentence, while 'rufen' stays in the second position
explanation_tr: anrufen' ayrılabilir bir fiildir. 'an' öneki cümlenin sonuna giderken, 'rufen' ikinci pozisyonda kalır

sentence_de: Ich habe gestern acht Stunden gearbeitet
translation_en: I worked eight hours yesterday
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The 'habe...ge-t' pattern shows an action that happened in the past. Many verbs use 'haben' for this pattern
explanation_tr: habe...ge-t' kalıbı geçmişte olan bir eylemi gösterir. Birçok fiil bu kalıp için 'haben' kullanır

sentence_de: Wo kann er eine Apotheke finden?
translation_en: Where can he find a pharmacy?
question_en: Which statement about "Apotheke" is correct?
explanation_en: Apotheke' is feminine (die Apotheke). The verb 'finden' requires an accusative object
explanation_tr: Apotheke' dişil bir isimdir (die Apotheke). 'finden' fiili akuzatif bir nesne gerektirir

sentence_de: Es gibt dort alte Gebäude
translation_en: There are old buildings there
question_en: What case does 'es gibt' require
explanation_en: Gebäude' is a plural noun, 'alt' takes the plural ending '-e'. 'es gibt' always forces the noun into accusative, no matter what comes after it
explanation_tr: Gebäude' çoğul olduğu için 'alt' sıfatı çoğul eki '-e' alır. 'es gibt' kendinden sonra gelen ismi her zaman akuzatif yapar

sentence_de: Hier ist das Rathaus
translation_en: Here is the town hall
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'Rathaus'?
explanation_en: In compound nouns the last word decides the gender. 'Haus' is neuter (das Haus), so 'Rathaus' is also neuter. Always look at the last piece; it is the boss of the whole word
explanation_tr: Birleşik kelimelerde son kelime cinsiyeti belirler. 'Haus' nötr olduğu için (das Haus), 'Rathaus' da nötrdür. Her zaman son parçaya bakın; tüm kelimenin patronu odur

sentence_de: Deutschland ist kein kleines Land
translation_en: Germany is not a small country
question_en: What is the case of the sentence?
explanation_en: The sentence describes a simple subject and is in the nominative case
explanation_tr: Cümle, basit bir özneyi açıklayan ve Yalın hâlde olan bir cümledir

sentence_de: Das Museum ist heute nicht geöffnet
translation_en: The museum is not open today
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'nicht'?
explanation_en: nicht' negates adjectives and verbs. Here it negates the adjective 'geöffnet'. To negate a noun you would use 'kein'
explanation_tr: nicht' sıfatları ve fiilleri olumsuz yapar. Burada 'geöffnet' sıfatını olumsuz yapar. İsimleri olumsuz yapmak için 'kein' kullanılır

sentence_de: Wo hat sie dieses Kleid gekauft?
translation_en: Where did she buy this dress?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The 'hat... ge-' pattern is the standard way to describe actions that happened in the past
explanation_tr: “Hat … ge-” kalıbı, geçmişte gerçekleşmiş eylemleri anlatmanın standart yoludur

sentence_de: Er hat den Apfelsaft gekauft
translation_en: He bought the apple juice
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Apfelsaft"?
explanation_en: Apfelsaft' is the direct object of 'kaufen' and is masculine, so it takes the accusative case 'den'
explanation_tr: Apfelsaft' fiilin doğrudan nesnesi olduğu için Akuzatifdir (belirtme hâli) ve eril bir isim olduğu için 'den' alır

sentence_de: Wir haben unser Auto im Autohaus verkauft
translation_en: We sold our car at the car dealership
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Autohaus"?
explanation_en: Because 'in' describes a fixed location, the word 'Autohaus' uses the dative case. 'in' is a two-way preposition; staying somewhere means dative, moving somewhere means accusative
explanation_tr: in' eylemin yapıldığı yeri (-de, -da) belirttiği için 'Autohaus' dativ hâlindedir. 'in' iki yönlü bir edattır; bir yerde durmak dativ, bir yere gitmek akuzatif demektir

sentence_de: Hast du einen Ausweis bei dir
translation_en: Do you have an ID with you?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "bei"?
explanation_en: The preposition 'bei' always requires the dative case. Some other prepositions that take the dative case are 'aus', 'bei', 'von', and 'zu'
explanation_tr: 'bei' edatı her zaman yönelme hâli (Dativ) gerektirir. 'aus', 'bei', 'von', 'zu' gibi edatlar daima Dativ alır

sentence_de: Das alte Fahrrad war billig
translation_en: The old bicycle was cheap
question_en: What does 'war' tell you about the time?
explanation_en: war' is the simple past (Präteritum) form of the verb 'sein'
explanation_tr: War' fiili, 'sein' (olmak) fiilinin basit geçmiş zaman (Präteritum) çekimidir ve geçmişte tamamlanmış bir durumu ifade eder

sentence_de: Hier ist sein Reisepass
translation_en: Here is his passport
question_en: Who is the subject in this sentence?
explanation_en: German sentence structure does not require the subject to be in the first position, but the verb must always be in the second position; here, the subject is “sein Reisepass”
explanation_tr: Almanca cümle yapısında öznenin birinci sırada olması bir zorunluluk değildir, ancak fiil her zaman ikinci sırada olmalıdır; burada özne 'sein Reisepass'tir

sentence_de: Geht sie am Samstagabend ins Kino?
translation_en: Is she going to the cinema on Saturday evening?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Kino"?
explanation_en: The sentence uses the accusative case. 'Kino' is a neuter noun, and 'ins' is the contraction of 'in das'
explanation_tr: Cümle, akuzatif halini kullanmaktadır. 'Kino' nötr bir isimdir ve 'ins', 'in das'ın kısaltmasıdır

sentence_de: München hat viele moderne Museen
translation_en: Munich has many modern museums
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Museen"?
explanation_en: “Museen” is plural, and “hat” takes an accusative object; the -e endings in “viele” and “moderne” show the plural form
explanation_tr: “Museen” çoğuldur ve “hat” fiili Akuzatif nesne alır; “viele” ve “moderne” kelimelerindeki -e ekleri çoğul ekidir

sentence_de: Sie müssen zum Arzt gehen
translation_en: You need to see a doctor
question_en: What role does 'müssen' play in this sentence?
question_tr: müssen' bu cümlede nasıl bir rol oynuyor?
explanation_en: In a sentence with 'müssen', the helper verb takes the 2nd spot, and the action verb 'gehen' moves to the very end
explanation_tr: müssen' (zorunda olmak) gibi modal fiiller ikinci sırada çekimlenir. Asıl fiil olan 'gehen' ise cümlenin sonuna yalın hâlde kalır

sentence_de: Ist das der Weg zum Bahnhof?
translation_en: Is this the way to the train station?
translation_tr: Tren istasyonuna giden yol bu mu?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "zum"?
explanation_en: The particle 'zu' always takes the dative case, and when the noun that follows is masculine or neuter, 'dem' is used, making 'zu dem' 'zum'
explanation_tr: Zu' edatı her zaman datif halini alır ve eğer ondan sonra gelen isim eril veya nötr ise 'dem' kullanılır ve 'zu dem' 'zum' olur

sentence_de: Der Bus nach Berlin fährt an der Ausfahrt 2 ab
translation_en: The bus to Berlin departs from exit 2
translation_tr: Berlin'e giden otobüs 2 numaralı çıkıştan kalkmaktadır
question_en: Where does the conjugated verb sit in this sentence?
question_tr: Çekimli fiil bu cümlede nerede duruyor?
explanation_en: In a simple main clause, the conjugated verb always stands in the second position. The subject or another element can be in the first position
explanation_tr: Basit bir cümlede, çekimli fiil her zaman ikinci konumda yer alır. Özne veya başka bir unsur birinci konumda olabilir

sentence_de: Sie lernt seit 5 Monaten in der Schule
translation_en: She has been attending school for 5 months
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the subject?
explanation_en: Since the verb 'lernt' is singular, the word 'sie' here means 'she,' not 'they.'
explanation_tr: Cümledeki 'lernt' fiil çekimi tekil olduğu için buradaki 'sie' kelimesi 'onlar' değil, 'o' (dişi) anlamına gelmektedir

sentence_de: Wo können wir ein Restaurant suchen?
translation_en: Where can we look for a restaurant?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "können"?
explanation_en: Modal verbs are conjugated in the second position, and the main verb (suchen) goes to the end in its infinitive form
explanation_tr: Modal fiiller çekimli hâlde 2. sırada yer alır, asıl fiil (suchen) ise mastar olarak cümlenin sonuna gider

sentence_de: Ich habe ein Kilo Birnen gekauft
translation_en: I bought a kilo of pears
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The 'habe...ge-t' pattern is used with 'haben' and a past participle to describe actions in the past
explanation_tr: habe...ge-t' yapısı, 'haben' yardımcı fiili ve geçmiş zaman ortacı ile geçmişte tamamlanmış eylemleri anlatır

sentence_de: Ich trinke meinen Kaffee
translation_en: I drink a coffee
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "meinen"?
explanation_en: The verb 'trinken' needs Akkusative. Masculine nouns change 'mein' to 'meinen' in accusative case
explanation_tr: trinken' fiili Belirtme hâli (Neyi?) ister. Eril isimlerde 'mein' kelimesi 'meinen' olur

sentence_de: Sie isst keine Butter
translation_en: She doesn't eat butter
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "keine"?
explanation_en: kein' replaces 'ein' to make a noun negative. 'kein' is used with '-e' ending for feminine nouns
explanation_tr: kein', 'ein' yerine gelerek isimleri olumsuz yapar. Dişil isimler için 'kein' sonuna '-e' alarak 'keine' olur

sentence_de: Holst du heute das Brot ab?
translation_en: Are you picking up the bread today?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
question_tr: Cümlenin fiili için aşağıdakilerden hangisi doğrudur?
explanation_en: Abholen' is a separable verb. 'Holst' is conjugated for 'du' and placed second, with 'ab' at the end of the sentence. Even in a question, the prefix still waits at the very end
explanation_tr: Abholen' ayrılabilir bir fiildir. 'du' öznesine göre çekimlenir ve 'holst' cümlenin ikinci sırasında, 'ab' ise sonunda yer alır. Soru cümlesinde bile önek yine en sondadır

sentence_de: Ich kann einen Kaffee machen
translation_en: I can make a coffee
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'Kaffee'?
explanation_en: The verb 'machen' requires the accusative case, and 'Kaffee' is a masculine noun, so 'ein' changes to 'einen'
explanation_tr: machen' fiili Akuzatif (belirtme -i) hâli gerektirir ve 'Kaffee' eril bir isimdir, bu yüzden 'ein' 'einen' olur

sentence_de: Welches Getränk mag er?
translation_en: What drink does he like?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Welches'?
explanation_en: Getränk' is neuter (das) and 'mag' requires accusative; so 'Welche' takes '-s' ending. 'Welch-' works like an article; it dresses up to match the gender of the noun it asks about
explanation_tr: Getränk' nötr (das) bir isimdir ve 'mag' fiili belirtme hâli (Akuzatif) gerektirir; bu nedenle 'Welche' sonuna '-s' alır. 'Welch-' bir artikel gibi çalışır; sorduğu ismin cinsiyetine göre şekil alır

sentence_de: Sie hat alle Äpfel gelegt
translation_en: She laid all the apples
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the subject?
explanation_en: The verb 'hat' is conjugated for a singular subject, indicating 'Sie' refers to 'she', which is third-person singular
explanation_tr: Fiil 'hat', tekil bir özne için çekimlenmiştir, bu da 'Sie'nin 'o' (kadın) anlamına geldiğini ve üçüncü tekil şahıs olduğunu gösterir

sentence_de: Er mag Brot mit Honig und Marmelade
translation_en: He likes bread with honey and jam
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'mag' is used with 'er' (he), 'sie' (she), or 'es' (it) when talking about singular person
explanation_tr: Fiil 'mag', 'er' (o erkek), 'sie' (o kadın) veya 'es' (o cansız) ile tekil kişiden bahsederken kullanılır

sentence_de: Wir essen oft Reis zum Abendessen
translation_en: We often eat rice for dinner
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "zum"?
explanation_en: zum' is a short way to say 'zu dem'. The word 'zu' always uses the Dative case
explanation_tr: zum', 'zu dem' kelimelerinin kısaltılmış hâlidir. 'zu' edatı her zaman Yönelme hâlini (Dativ) kullanır

sentence_de: Ich esse manchmal eine Banane
translation_en: I sometimes eat a banana
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Banane"?
explanation_en: Many nouns ending in -e are feminine, like 'Banane'. This means its definite article is 'die' and its indefinite article is 'eine'
explanation_tr: -e ile biten birçok isim dişildir. Bu yüzden belirli artikeli 'die', belirsiz artikeli ise 'eine' olur

sentence_de: Er hatte Brot und Käse zum Frühstück
translation_en: He had bread and cheese for breakfast
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: hatte' is the past form of 'haben'
explanation_tr: hatte', 'haben' fiilinin geçmiş zaman hâlidir

sentence_de: Er isst Reis und Obst
translation_en: He eats rice and fruit
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'essen' changes its vowel from 'e' to 'i' for 'du' and 'er/sie/es' forms. This makes 'isst' an irregular verb. Some verbs surprise you by changing their core; 'essen' is one of the common ones
explanation_tr: essen' fiili, 'du' ve 'er/sie/es' için ünlüsünü 'e'den 'i'ye değiştirir. Bu yüzden 'isst' düzensiz bir fiildir. Bazı fiiller özlerini değiştirerek sizi şaşırtır; 'essen' en yaygınlardan biridir

sentence_de: Ich mag keine Nudeln
translation_en: I don't like noodles
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "keine"?
explanation_en: keine' negates the noun 'Nudeln'. The 'kein' pattern is used for nouns. 'nicht' negates verbs or adjectives. 'Nudeln' is plural, so 'kein' takes '-e' just like all plural nouns require
explanation_tr: keine' 'Nudeln' ismini olumsuz yapar. 'kein' kalıbı isimler için kullanılır. Fiilleri veya sıfatları 'nicht' olumsuz yapar. 'Nudeln' çoğuldur, bu yüzden 'kein' tüm çoğul isimlerin gerektirdiği gibi '-e' alır

sentence_de: Wir brauchen drei Tomaten
translation_en: We need three tomatoes
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Tomaten"?
explanation_en: The number 'drei' shows there is more than one 'Tomate', making 'Tomaten' a plural noun by adding the '-n' ending
explanation_tr: drei' sayısı, birden fazla 'Tomate' olduğunu gösterir; bu nedenle '-n' eki eklenerek 'Tomaten' çoğu hâle getirilir

sentence_de: Darf ich ein Stück Kuchen nehmen?
translation_en: May I take a piece of cake?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: darf' is a modal verb conjugated for 'ich'. The main verb 'nehmen' goes to the end in its infinitive form. Same form every time; modal verb does the grammar up front, main verb carries the action at the end
explanation_tr: darf' modal fiil olup 'ich' için çekimlenmiştir. Ana fiil 'nehmen' ise mastar hâlinde cümlenin sonuna gider. Her seferinde aynı kural; modal fiil önde grameri yapar, asıl fiil sonda eylemi taşır

sentence_de: Nehmen Sie einen Joghurt?
translation_en: Would you like some yogurt?
question_en: What is the case of the sentence?
explanation_en: Because 'Joghurt' is the direct object (what you take), it uses the Akkusativ case. 'ein' changes to 'einen' for masculine nouns
explanation_tr: Joghurt' nesne olduğu için (neyi alıyorsunuz?) akuzatif hâlindedir. 'ein' eril isimler için 'einen' olarak değişir

sentence_de: Mögen Sie noch eine Torte
translation_en: Would you like another cake?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the subject?
explanation_en: The capitalized 'Sie' with the verb 'mögen' shows you are speaking to someone formally. This 'Sie' means 'you'
explanation_tr: Büyük harfle yazılan 'Sie' ve 'mögen' fiilinin çekimi, birine resmi bir şekilde hitap ettiğinizi gösterir. Bu 'Sie', 'siz' demektir

sentence_de: Wir bekommen eine Flasche Mineralwasser
translation_en: We get a bottle of mineral water
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Mineralwasser"?
explanation_en: The last word, 'Wasser', decides the gender of 'Mineralwasser'. Because 'das Wasser' is neuter, 'Mineralwasser' is also neuter
explanation_tr: Mineralwasser' kelimesinde son kelime olan 'Wasser' cinsiyeti belirler. 'das Wasser' nötr olduğu için 'Mineralwasser' da nötrdür

sentence_de: Ihr braucht einen Löffel
translation_en: You will need a spoon
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the subject?
explanation_en: Ihr' shows who needs the spoon. It is the plural form of 'you' when talking to more than one person. Do not confuse it with 'ihr' meaning 'her'; context and verb ending will always tell you which one
explanation_tr: Ihr'; birden fazla kişiye 'siz' derken kullanılan çoğul hâldir. 'ihr' aynı zamanda 'onun (dişil)' anlamına da gelir; hangisi olduğunu bağlam ve fiil takısı her zaman söyler

sentence_de: Ich gehe ins Café
translation_en: I'm going to the cafe
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Café"?
explanation_en: Since 'café' is neutral (das Café), 'das' remains the same in the accusative case and combines with 'in' to become 'ins'
explanation_tr: Café' nötrdür (das Café), 'das' belirtme hâlinde aynı kalır ve 'in' ile birleşerek 'ins' olur

sentence_de: Wie ist das Wetter dort?
translation_en: What's the weather like there?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Wetter"?
explanation_en: The word 'Wetter' uses 'das' as its article. This pattern shows it is a neuter noun
explanation_tr: Wetter' kelimesi 'das' artikeli ile kullanılır. Bu kalıp, onun nötr bir isim olduğunu gösterir

sentence_de: Der Pazifik ist sehr groß
translation_en: The Pacific Ocean is very large
question_en: What case is used in the sentence?
explanation_en: Sentences that describe the subject use the nominative case. Here, ist (sein) is the verb
explanation_tr: Özne hakkında bilgi veren cümleler nominatif hâl kullanır. Burada fiil ist (sein) dir

sentence_de: Ich zelte im Nationalpark
translation_en: I'm camping in the national park
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Nationalpark"?
explanation_en: The word 'im' is a short form of 'in dem'. 'dem' comes from 'der', which shows 'Nationalpark' is a masculine noun. 'in' becomes 'im' when you are staying somewhere; if you were going there, it would be 'in den'
explanation_tr: im' kelimesi 'in dem'in kısa hâlidir. 'dem', 'der'in dativ hâlidir ve bu da 'Nationalpark'ın eril bir isim olduğunu gösterir. Bir yerde duruyorsanız 'in' 'im' olur; oraya gidiyor olsaydınız 'in den' olurdu

sentence_de: Sie sitzt im Garten
translation_en: She is sitting in the garden
question_en: Why is 'im' used here instead of 'in'?
explanation_en: When 'in' refers to a place where something stays, it requires dative. 'der Garten' becomes 'dem Garten', and 'in' + 'dem' squeeze together into 'im'. Staying = dative, moving = accusative
explanation_tr: in' bir yerde bulunmayı ifade ettiğinde dativ gerektirir. 'der Garten' 'dem Garten' olur ve 'in' + 'dem' birleşerek 'im' olur. Kalmak = dativ, gitmek = akuzatif

sentence_de: Die Sonne geht um 19 Uhr unter
translation_en: The sun sets at 7 pm
question_en: Does this verb stay together or split apart?
question_tr: Buradaki fiil bir arada mı kalır yoksa ayrılır mı?
explanation_en: untergehen' is a separable verb. 'geht' stays in second position and the prefix 'unter' goes to the end of the sentence
explanation_tr: untergehen' ayrılabilir bir fiildir. 'geht' ikinci sırada kalır ve 'unter' öneki cümlenin sonuna gider

sentence_de: Das Wetter war wunderbar diesen Sommer
translation_en: The weather was wonderful this summer
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Sommer"?
explanation_en: The word 'diesen' before 'Sommer' shows it is masculine. 'dieser' family works like 'der'; it dresses up to match gender and case. Here '-en' signals masculine accusative
explanation_tr: Sommer' kelimesinden önce gelen 'diesen' kelimesi onun eril olduğunu gösterir. 'dieser' ailesi 'der' gibi çalışır; cinsiyet ve hâle göre şekil alır. Burada '-en' eril akuzatifi işaret eder

sentence_de: Der Baum hat grüne Blätter
translation_en: The tree has green leaves
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'haben' is irregular. It changes its form for 'du' (hast) and 'er/sie/es' (hat) instead of just adding regular endings
explanation_tr: ‘haben’ fiili düzensizdir. Düzenli ekler almak yerine ‘du’ için 'hast', ‘er/sie/es’ için 'hat' şeklinde değişir

sentence_de: Ich mag Katzen
translation_en: I like cats
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Katzen"?
explanation_en: The word 'Katzen' shows many cats. It uses the plural ending -n, which is common for feminine nouns
explanation_tr: Katze' kelimesi, sonuna '-n' takısı alarak 'Katzen' olur ve çoğul yapılır; bu, dişil kelimeler için en yaygın çoğul yapma yöntemidir

sentence_de: Gestern Abend war es windig
translation_en: It was windy last night
question_en: Which word is actually the subject here?
explanation_en: When a time like 'Gestern Abend' is first, the subject 'es' then comes after the verb. The verb is always in second position
explanation_tr: Gestern Abend' gibi bir zaman ifadesi başta olduğunda, 'es' öznesi fiilden sonra gelir. Fiil her zaman 2. pozisyondadır

sentence_de: Die Temperatur liegt bei 20 Grad
translation_en: The temperature is 20 degrees
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "bei"?
explanation_en: bei' is one of the prepositions that always needs the Dativ case. Others in this group include 'mit, zu, nach, von, and aus'
explanation_tr: bei', her zaman Dativ hâl gerektiren edatlardan biridir. 'mit, zu, bei, nach, von, aus' bu gruba ait diğer edatlardır

sentence_de: Das Wetter ist sehr sonnig
translation_en: The weather is very sunny
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Wetter"?
explanation_en: The word 'Wetter' uses 'das' before it. Nouns that use 'das' are neuter
explanation_tr: Wetter' kelimesinin önünde 'das' vardır. 'Das' alan isimler nötrdür

sentence_de: Es ist ein ruhiger Wald
translation_en: It is a quiet forest
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Wald"?
explanation_en: The article 'ein' and the adjective ending '-er' in 'ruhiger'  show that 'Wald' is masculine
explanation_tr: Belirsiz artikel 'ein' ve sıfatın '-er' eki (ruhiger), 'Wald' kelimesinin eril olduğunu gösterir

sentence_de: Es war nicht so kalt im Winter
translation_en: It wasn't that cold in winter
translation_tr: Kışın o kadar da soğuk değildi
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Winter"?
explanation_en: Because 'im' (in dem) shows a location, 'Winter' is in the Dative case. Seasons always use 'im'; im Winter, im Sommer, im Frühling, im Herbst
explanation_tr: im' (in dem) bir yer belirttiği için 'Winter' dativ hâldedir. Mevsimler her zaman 'im' ile kullanılır; im Winter, im Sommer, im Frühling, im Herbst

sentence_de: Wie heißt der See?
translation_en: What is the name of the lake?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'heißen' follows the regular pattern. For 'der See' (which is like 'er'), the verb ending is '-t'
explanation_tr: heißen' fiili düzenli çekim kuralına uyar. Özne 'der See' (er) olduğu için fiil '-t' takısı alır

sentence_de: Es hat die ganze Nacht geregnet
translation_en: It rained all night
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The 'hat... ge-t' pattern shows a past action
explanation_tr: hat... ge-t' kalıbı geçmişte olan bir eylemi gösterir

sentence_de: Grönland ist eine Insel mit viel Eis
translation_en: Greenland is an island with a lot of ice
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Eis"?
explanation_en: The word 'mit' always uses the Dativ case for the noun that follows it, just like 'bei', 'von', 'zu', 'nach', 'aus', 'seit'
explanation_tr: mit' edatı kendinden sonraki ismi her zaman Dativ yapar, tıpkı 'bei', 'von', 'zu', 'nach', 'aus', 'seit' gibi

sentence_de: Ihr Geburtstag ist im Winter
translation_en: Her birthday is in winter
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Winter"?
explanation_en: The dative case is used to express location, where 'der' becomes 'dem'; therefore, 'im' serves as the shortened form of 'in dem'. Seasons always go with 'im'; im Winter, im Sommer, im Frühling, im Herbst
explanation_tr: Yer bildirildiği için dative hâl kullanılır ve 'der' artikeli 'dem'e dönüşür; 'im' ise bu dative yapıdaki 'in dem' ifadesinin kısaltılmış hâlidir. Mevsimler her zaman 'im' ile kullanılır; im Winter, im Sommer, im Frühling, im Herbst

sentence_de: Ich lerne seit drei Monaten Deutsch
translation_en: I have been learning German for three months
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "seit"?
explanation_en: Seit' tells you how long something has been happening. The words after 'seit' always use the Dative case
explanation_tr: Seit' bir şeyin ne kadar süredir olduğunu gösterir. 'Seit' kelimesinden sonraki kelimeler her zaman dativ hâlindedir

sentence_de: Der Bus kommt um 10 Uhr an
translation_en: The bus arrives at 10 o'clock
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "um"?
explanation_en: The word 'um' always comes before a specific clock time (Uhr)
explanation_tr: Saat belirtmek için kullanılan edat 'um'dur

sentence_de: Wir haben ihn im Herbst kennengelernt
translation_en: We met him in the fall
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The 'haben...ge-t' pattern shows a past action. Here, 'haben' and 'kennengelernt' together form the past tense
explanation_tr: haben...ge-t' kalıbı geçmişte olan bir eylemi gösterir. Burada 'haben' ve 'kennengelernt' geçmiş zamanı ifade eder

sentence_de: Im August trinken sie viel Wasser
translation_en: They drink a lot of water in August
question_en: Which word is actually the subject here?
explanation_en: The subject can change position; here it's in 3rd place. The verb 'trinken' ends with '-en,' matching 'sie'
explanation_tr: Özne yer değiştirebilir; burada 3. pozisyondadır. Fiil 'trinken' '-en' ile biter, bu da 'sie' (çoğul) ile uyumludur

sentence_de: Der Mai ist ein herrlicher Monat
translation_en: May is a wonderful month
question_en: Which word is actually the subject here
explanation_en: The verb 'ist' (to be) always defines something about the subject, and that’s why it uses the Nominative case
explanation_tr: ist' (to be) fiili her zaman özneyi tanımlar ve bu yüzden Nominativdir

sentence_de: Am Freitag verschenkt er meine Bücher
translation_en: He's giving away my books on Friday
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "am"?
option_2_en: Used for all the time expressions
explanation_en: The pattern 'am' is used with days. 'um' is for clock times, and 'im' is for months and seasons
explanation_tr: am' günler ile kullanılır. Saatler için 'um', aylar ve mevsimler için 'im' kullanılır

sentence_de: Montags fährt sie zur Arbeit
translation_en: She goes to work on Mondays
question_en: Is 'sie' one person or many here?
question_tr: Buradaki 'sie' tek kişi mi yoksa birden fazla mı?
explanation_en: Sie' can mean 'she' (verb ends in -t), 'they' (verb ends in -en), or formal 'you' (capitalized, verb ends in -en). The verb ending is your detective; it solves the mystery of which 'sie' you are reading
explanation_tr: sie', kullanılan fiil çekimine bağlı olarak 'dişil o' (fiil -t ile biter), 'onlar' (fiil -en ile biter) veya 'siz' (büyük harfle yazılır, fiil -en ile biter) anlamına gelebilir. Fiil takısı dedektifinizdir; hangi 'sie' ile karşılaştığınızı söyler

sentence_de: Wir können am Mittwoch nicht ins Kino gehen
translation_en: We can't go to the cinema on Wednesday
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "am"?
explanation_en: The pattern 'am' is used with days (am Mittwoch). 'um' is for clock times (um 8 Uhr). 'im' is for months and seasons (im Mai, im Sommer)
explanation_tr: “am” kalıbı günler ile kullanılır (am Mittwoch). “um” saatlerde kullanılır (um 8 Uhr). “im” aylar ve mevsimler için kullanılır (im Mai, im Sommer)

sentence_de: Ich gehe am Mittag in den Park
translation_en: I'm going to the park at noon
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Park"?
explanation_en: The article 'den' shows that 'Park' is masculine and in the accusative case. When there is a movement, it becomes accusative case
explanation_tr: den' artikeli, 'Park' kelimesinin eril olduğunu ve akuzatif (ismin -i hali) formunda kullanıldığını gösterir; bir yönelme veya hareket olduğunda akuzatif hâli kullanılır

sentence_de: Sie stehen spät auf
translation_en: They get up late
question_en: Does this verb stay together or split apart?
question_tr: Buradaki fiil bir arada mı kalır yoksa ayrılır mı?
explanation_en: The verb 'aufstehen' is separable, meaning the prefix 'auf' goes to the very end of the sentence. No matter how many words sit between the verb and its prefix, they always find each other at the end
explanation_tr: aufstehen' ayrılabilir bir fiildir; bu yapıda 'auf' ön eki cümlenin sonuna taşınır. Fiil ile ön eki arasına kaç kelime girerse girsin, sonda her zaman birbirlerini bulurlar

sentence_de: Was machst du nächsten Freitag?
translation_en: What are you doing next Friday?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "Freitag"?
explanation_en: Days of the weeks are always masculine and use 'der'
explanation_tr: Haftanın günleri her zaman erildir ve 'der' kullanır

sentence_de: Sie hat im Sommer keine Termine
translation_en: She has no appointments in the summer
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "keine"?
explanation_en: Keine' is used because 'Termine' is a noun, meaning 'no appointments'. In contrast, 'nicht' is negating verbs or adjectives
explanation_tr: Termine' bir isim olduğu için ''keine' kullanılır; 'nicht' ise sadece fiilleri veya sıfatları olumsuz yapmak içindir

sentence_de: Um wie viel Uhr hat er angerufen?
translation_en: What time did he call?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for "um"?
explanation_en: um' used for clock times, 'am' is for days, and 'im' is for months or seasons
explanation_tr: um' saat için kullanılır, günler için 'am', aylar veya mevsimler için 'im' kullanılır

sentence_de: Ihr kommt im Sommer sehr spät an
translation_en: You arrive very late in the summer
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb 'ankommen' splits: 'kommt' stays with 'ihr', and 'an' goes to the end of the sentence. This is the pattern for separable verbs. By now you have seen 'aufstehen', 'anrufen', 'aussehen'; they all follow the same rule
explanation_tr: ankommen' fiili ayrılır: 'kommt' özneyle kalır, 'an' cümlenin sonuna gider. Ayrılabilir fiillerin kuralı budur. Şimdiye kadar 'aufstehen', 'anrufen', 'aussehen' gördünüz; hepsi aynı kurala uyar

sentence_de: Ihr habt nicht genug Zeit
translation_en: You don't have enough time
question_en: Is 'ihr' one person or many?
question_tr: ihr' tek kişi mi yoksa birden fazla mı?
explanation_en: Ihr' is the plural 'you' (you all), and it is the subject, so it is the person doing the action
explanation_tr: Ihr', 'siz' anlamına gelen kişi zamiridir. Cümlenin öznesi olduğu için Yalın hâldedir (Nominatif)

sentence_de: Das ist meine Mutter
translation_en: This is my mother
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'meine'?
explanation_en: Mutter' is a feminine noun (die Mutter), so the possessive 'mein' takes '-e' to become 'meine'
explanation_tr: Mutter' dişil bir isimdir (die Mutter). Bu yüzden 'mein' iyelik zamiri '-e' takısı alarak 'meine' olur

sentence_de: Ich habe einen Bruder
translation_en: I have a brother
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'Bruder'?
explanation_en: haben' takes a direct object, so 'Bruder' is in the accusative case. 'Bruder' is masculine, so 'ein' changes to 'einen'. Every time you see 'einen', a masculine noun is receiving the action
explanation_tr: haben' fiili akuzatif nesne gerektirir. 'Bruder' eril olduğu için 'ein' 'einen' olur. 'einen' gördüğünüz her seferde, eril bir isim eylemin hedefi oluyor demektir

sentence_de: Mein Vater ist Arzt
translation_en: My father is a doctor
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'mein'?
explanation_en: Vater' is a masculine noun (der Vater), so the possessive takes no extra ending, stays 'mein'
explanation_tr: Vater' eril bir isimdir (der Vater). Bu yüzden iyelik zamiri ek almaz ve 'mein' olarak kalır

sentence_de: Wir haben zwei Kinder
translation_en: We have two children
question_en: Is 'Kinder' one or many?
question_tr: Kinder' tek mi yoksa birden fazla mı?
explanation_en: Kinder' is the plural of 'Kind'. All plural nouns use 'die' as their article
explanation_tr: Kinder', 'Kind' kelimesinin çoğuludur. Tüm çoğul isimler 'die' artikeli alır

sentence_de: Meine Schwester wohnt in Berlin
translation_en: My sister lives in Berlin
question_en: What does the verb 'wohnt' tell you about the subject?
question_tr: wohnt' fiili özne hakkında ne söylüyor?
explanation_en: wohnt' ends in '-t' which shows it is conjugated for 'er/sie/es'. Here 'meine Schwester' is third-person singular
explanation_tr: wohnt' fiili '-t' ekiyle biter; bu 'er/sie/es' için çekildiğini gösterir. 'meine Schwester' üçüncü tekil şahıstır

sentence_de: Er hat keine Geschwister
translation_en: He has no siblings
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'keine'?
explanation_en: Geschwister' is a plural noun, so 'kein' takes '-e' to become 'keine'. To negate a verb, 'nicht' is used
explanation_tr: Geschwister' çoğul bir isimdir. Bu yüzden 'kein' sonuna '-e' alır. Fiilleri olumsuz yapmak için 'nicht' kullanılır

sentence_de: Meine Eltern leben in Istanbul
translation_en: My parents live in Istanbul
question_en: Can 'Eltern' ever be singular?
explanation_en: Eltern' (parents) is always plural and it has no singular form. The verb 'leben' confirms this with its '-en' plural ending. Some words exist only in plural
explanation_tr: Eltern' kelimesi her zaman çoğuldur, tekil hâli yoktur. 'leben' fiilinin '-en' eki de bunu doğrular. Bazı kelimeler sadece çoğul olarak var olur

sentence_de: Mein Bruder hat gestern angerufen
translation_en: My brother called yesterday
question_en: What is happening with the verb 'anrufen' here?
option_2_en: One piece and in the past tense
option_2_tr: Tek parça hâlde ve geçmiş zamanda
explanation_en: anrufen' is a separable verb. In the Perfekt tense, the prefix 'an' attaches directly to the participle: 'angerufen'. Separable verbs split in the present but reunite in the past
explanation_tr: anrufen' ayrılabilir bir fiildir. Geçmiş zamanda 'an' ön eki fiilin son hâline doğrudan eklenir: 'angerufen'. Ayrılabilir fiiller şimdiki zamanda ayrılır ama geçmiş zamanda birleşir

sentence_de: Ich wohne bei meinen Eltern
translation_en: I live with my parents
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'bei'?
explanation_en: bei' always requires the dative case. 'meine Eltern' becomes 'meinen Eltern' in the dative plural
explanation_tr: bei' edatı her zaman dativ gerektirir. 'meine Eltern' dativ çoğulda 'meinen Eltern' olur

sentence_de: Meine Tante ist die Schwester meiner Mutter
translation_en: My aunt is my mother's sister
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'meiner'?
explanation_en: Mutter' is feminine. Genitive answers the question 'whose?'; here, whose sister is she? My mother's. 'mein' takes '-er' for feminine genitive
explanation_tr: Mutter' dişildir. Genitif 'kimin?' sorusunu yanıtlar; burada, kimin kız kardeşi? Annemin. Dişil sahiplik bildirirken 'mein' '-er' takısı alır

sentence_de: Ihr Sohn ist sehr intelligent
translation_en: Her son is very intelligent
question_en: Does 'ihr' mean 'her' or 'their' here?
explanation_en: ihr' here is the possessive pronoun for 'sie' (she), meaning 'her'. 'Sohn' is masculine so 'ihr' takes no extra ending
explanation_tr: Buradaki 'ihr', 'sie' (o, dişil) için iyelik zamiridir ve 'onun' anlamına gelir. 'Sohn' eril olduğu için 'ihr' ek almaz

sentence_de: Mit wem wohnst du zusammen?
translation_en: Who do you live with?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'mit'?
explanation_en: mit' always requires the dative case. 'wem' is the dative form of 'wer' (who)
explanation_tr: mit' edatı her zaman dativ gerektirir. 'wem', 'wer' kelimesinin dativ hâlidir

sentence_de: Meine Mutter hat mir ein Geschenk gegeben
translation_en: My mother gave me a gift
question_en: Why is 'mir' used instead of 'mich'?
explanation_en: geben' takes two objects: 'mir' (to whom, dative) and 'ein Geschenk' (what, accusative). 'ich' becomes 'mir' in the dative. Two objects, two cases; the person receiving gets dative, the thing being given gets accusative
explanation_tr: geben' iki nesne alır: 'mir' (kime, dativ) ve 'ein Geschenk' (neyi, akuzatif). 'ich' zamiri dativde 'mir' olur. İki nesne, iki hâl; alan kişi dativ, verilen şey akuzatif olur

sentence_de: Der Bruder meines Freundes heißt Thomas
translation_en: My friend's brother is called Thomas
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'meines Freundes'?
explanation_en: The genitive shows belonging. For masculine nouns, 'mein' becomes 'meines' and the noun takes '-s': 'Freundes'. The '-s' on the noun is the fingerprint of masculine genitive
explanation_tr: Genitif sahipliği gösterir. Eril isimlerde 'mein' 'meines' olur ve isim '-s' takısı alır: 'Freundes'. İsimdeki '-s', eril genitifin parmak izidir

sentence_de: Meine Eltern haben mir erlaubt, allein zu reisen
translation_en: My parents allowed me to travel alone
translation_tr: Ailem yalnız seyahat etmeme izin verdi
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'mir'?
explanation_en: erlauben' takes a dative object (to whom permission is given). 'ich' becomes 'mir' in the dative case
explanation_tr: erlauben' fiili dativ nesne gerektirir (kime izin veriliyor). 'ich' zamiri dativ hâlde 'mir' olur

sentence_de: Ich gehe in die Apotheke
translation_en: I am going to the pharmacy
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'in die'?
explanation_en: When 'in' shows movement toward a place, it takes the accusative. 'Apotheke' is feminine, so 'die' stays 'die' in accusative
explanation_tr: in' hareket gösterdiğinde akuzatif alır (yönelme hâli). 'Apotheke' dişil olduğu için akuzatifte 'die' değişmez

sentence_de: Ich fühle mich heute sehr müde
translation_en: I feel very tired today
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: fühlen' needs the reflexive pronoun 'mich' to express how the subject feels. This is the reflexive verb pattern: 'sich fühlen'
explanation_tr: fühlen' fiili öznenin nasıl hissettiğini ifade etmek için 'mich' dönüşlü zamirini gerektirir. Bu reflexive kalıbıdır 'sich fühlen'

sentence_de: Ich habe Schmerzen in der Schulter
translation_en: I have pain in my shoulder
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'in der'?
explanation_en: in der' shows where the pain is located, not movement. 'Schulter' is feminine, and 'die' becomes 'der' in the dative
explanation_tr: in der' ağrının nerede olduğunu gösterir, hareket değil. 'Schulter' dişildir ve dativde 'die' 'der' olur

sentence_de: Letzte Woche war ich beim Zahnarzt
translation_en: Last week I was at the dentist
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: war' is the Präteritum of 'sein'. 'sein' and 'haben' use Präteritum even in spoken German
explanation_tr: war', 'sein' fiilinin basit geçmiş zaman hâlidir. 'sein' ve 'haben' konuşma dilinde de kullanılır

sentence_de: Mein Vater war drei Tage im Krankenhaus
translation_en: My father was in hospital for three days
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'im Krankenhaus'?
explanation_en: im Krankenhaus' shows where he stayed (dativ), not movement. 'das Krankenhaus' in dativ becomes 'in dem' which contracts to 'im'
explanation_tr: im Krankenhaus' konum belirtir (dativ), hareket değil. 'das Krankenhaus' dativde 'in dem' olur ve 'im' olarak kısalır

sentence_de: Gestern hatte ich hohes Fieber
translation_en: Yesterday I had a high fever
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: hatte' is the Präteritum of 'haben'. Like 'sein', 'haben' uses this paste tense form (Präteritum) in everyday speech
explanation_tr: hatte', 'haben' fiilinin Präteritum hâlidir. 'sein' gibi, 'haben' de günlük konuşmada bu geçmiş zaman formunu (Präteritum) kullanır

sentence_de: Diese Salbe hilft sehr gut
translation_en: This ointment helps very well
translation_tr: Bu merhem çok iyi yardımcı oluyor
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'diese'?
explanation_en: diese' matches 'Salbe' which is feminine (die Salbe). As the subject, it is in the nominative case
explanation_tr: diese' sonuna '-e- alır, çünkü 'Salbe' dişildir (die Salbe). Özne olduğundan dolayı nominatif hâldedir

sentence_de: Welche Tabletten soll ich nehmen?
translation_en: Which tablets should I take?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'welche'?
explanation_en: Tabletten' is plural because of '-en' ending and the object of 'nehmen', so it is in the accusative. 'welche' takes no extra ending for plural accusative
explanation_tr: Tabletten' sonundaki '-en' ekinden dolayı çoğuldur ve 'nehmen' fiilinin nesnesidir, bu yüzden akuzatiftir. Çoğul akuzatifte 'welche' ek almaz

sentence_de: Wie fühlen Sie sich heute?
translation_en: How are you feeling today?
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'sich'?
explanation_en: sich fühlen' is a reflexive verb that expresses a person’s own emotional or physical state; 'sich' shows that the action refers back to the subject
explanation_tr: sich fühlen' kişinin kendi duygusal veya fiziksel durumunu ifade eden dönüşlü bir fiildir; burada 'sich', eylemin özneye geri döndüğünü gösterir

sentence_de: Mein Termin ist am dritten April
translation_en: My appointment is on the third of April
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'dritten'?
explanation_en: am' is 'an dem' and dative. Ordinal numbers take the '-ten' or '-sten' ending. 'dritte' becomes 'dritten' in dative
explanation_tr: am', 'an dem'dir ve datifdir. Sıra sayıları '-ten' veya '-sten' ekini alır. 'dritte', dativde 'dritten' olur

sentence_de: Nehmen Sie die Tabletten dreimal täglich
translation_en: Take the tablets three times a day
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: The verb comes first, followed by 'Sie'. This is the standard polite command form
explanation_tr: Fiil önce gelir, ardından 'Sie' eklenir. Bu standart kibar emir kalıbıdır

sentence_de: Ich war zum ersten Mal beim Hautarzt
translation_en: I was at the dermatologist for the first time
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'zum ersten Mal'?
question_tr: Aşağıdakilerden hangisi 'zum ersten Mal' için doğrudur?
explanation_en: zum' is 'zu dem', dative, and 'ersten' is the ordinal in dative form
explanation_tr: zum', 'zu dem' in kısaltmasıdır. 'ersten' ise sıra sayısının dativ hâlidir

sentence_de: Ich bin gestern zum Arzt gegangen
translation_en: I went to the doctor yesterday
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: Movement verbs uses 'sein' in the Perfekt. The participle 'gegangen' goes to the end
explanation_tr: Hareket bildiren fiiller 'sein' ile kullanılır. 'gegangen' cümlenin sonuna gider

sentence_de: Meine Mutter ist ins Krankenhaus gefahren
translation_en: My mother drove to the hospital
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'ins'?
explanation_en: fahren' shows movement, so 'in' takes the accusative. 'ins' is the contraction of 'in das', neuter accusative
explanation_tr: fahren' hareket gösterir, bu yüzden 'in' akuzatif alır. 'ins', 'in das'ın kısaltmasıdır, nötr akuzatif

sentence_de: Er ist traurig, weil sein Hund krank ist
translation_en: He is sad because his dog is sick
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'weil'?
explanation_en: weil' is a subordinating conjunction that sends the verb to the end of the sentence. Here 'ist' moves to the end
explanation_tr: weil' bağlaçtır ve fiili yan cümlenin sonuna gönderir. Bu yüzden 'ist' fiili en sona taşınır

sentence_de: Der Arzt klebt ein Pflaster auf die Wunde
translation_en: The doctor sticks a plaster on the wound
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'auf die'?
explanation_en: klebt...auf' shows a movement, so accusative. 'Wunde' is feminine, 'die' stays 'die' in accusative
explanation_tr: klebt...auf' hareket gösterir, yani akuzatif. 'Wunde' dişildir, akuzatifte 'die' değişmez

sentence_de: Heute geht es mir besser als gestern
translation_en: Today I feel better than yesterday
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'besser'?
explanation_en: besser' is the comparative form of 'gut'. Comparatives are formed by adding '-er','gut' is irregular, it becomes 'besser'
explanation_tr: besser', 'gut'un karşılaştırma derecesidir. Karşılaştırma '-er' eki ile yapılır. 'gut' düzensizdir, 'besser' olur

sentence_de: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich krank bin
translation_en: I am staying at home because I am sick
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'weil'?
explanation_en: weil' is a subordinating conjunction. It sends the verb to the very end of the sentence. Here 'bin' moves to the end
explanation_tr: weil' bağlaçtır ve fiili cümlenin sonuna atar. Bu yüzden 'bin' cümlenin sonundadır

sentence_de: Ich rufe dich morgen an
translation_en: I will call you tomorrow
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: In German, some verbs split in two. The prefix jumps to the end while the main verb stays in position 2.
explanation_tr: Almancada bazı fiiller ikiye ayrılır. Önek cümlenin sonuna giderken asıl fiil 2. sırada kalır

sentence_de: Ich schicke dir eine Nachricht
translation_en: I am sending you a message
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'dir'?
explanation_en: When a sentence has two objects, ask two questions: 'what am I sending?' (eine Nachricht, accusative) and 'to whom?' (dir, dative)
explanation_tr: Bir cümlede iki nesne varken iki soru sorulur: 'neyi gönderiyorum?' (eine Nachricht, akuzatif) ve 'kime?' (dir, dativ)

sentence_de: Wir sehen am Abend oft fern
translation_en: We often watch TV in the evening
question_en: Which of the following is correct for the verb?
explanation_en: fernsehen' splits just like 'anrufen', 'sehen' stays in position 2 and 'fern' goes to the end. Notice the vowel change too: it would be 'siehst' for 'du'
explanation_tr: fernsehen' de 'anrufen' gibi ayrılır — 'sehen' 2. sırada kalır, 'fern' sona gider. Ayrıca ünlü değişimine de dikkat: 'du' için 'siehst' olur

sentence_de: Mein Handy liegt auf dem Tisch
translation_en: My phone is on the table
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'auf dem'?
explanation_en: Two-way prepositions like 'auf' ask one simple question: is something staying (dative) or moving (accusative)? 'liegt' means it is sitting still, so dative. 'Tisch' is masculine, 'der' becomes 'dem' in dative
explanation_tr: auf' gibi iki yönlü edatlar tek bir soru sorar: bir yerde mi duruyor yoksa bir yere mi gidiyor? 'liegt' durmayı ifade eder, yani dativdir. 'Tisch' erildir, dativde 'der' 'dem' olur. Türkçedeki '-de' eki gibi; 'masada'

sentence_de: Ich habe ein neues Smartphone
translation_en: I have a new smartphone
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'neues'?
explanation_en: When an adjective comes after 'ein', it shows the gender; for neuter nouns it takes '-es' because 'ein' alone does not clearly show the gender, so 'neues' makes it clear
explanation_tr: ‘ein’ tek başına cinsiyeti gösteremez, bu yüzden sıfat devreye girer ve nötr isimlerde '-es' alır, böylece cinsiyet netleşir

sentence_de: Du darfst hier nicht telefonieren
translation_en: You are not allowed to phone here
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'nicht'?
explanation_en: nicht' negates verbs and actions; the act of phoning is forbidden. 'kein' negates a noun instead
explanation_tr: nicht' fiil ve eylemleri olumsuz yapar, burada telefon etme eylemi yasaklanmıştır. Bir ismi olumsuz yapmak için 'kein' kullanılır

sentence_de: Früher hatten wir kein Internet
translation_en: We didn't have internet before
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'hatten'?
explanation_en: haben' and 'sein' almost always use simple past (Präteritum) in spoken German. 'hatten' is the Präteritum of 'haben' for 'wir'
explanation_tr: haben' ve 'sein' fiilleri konuşma dilinde neredeyse her zaman basit geçmiş zaman (Präteritum) kullanır. 'hatten', 'haben' fiilinin 'wir' için basit geçmiş zaman hâlidir

sentence_de: Das Radio ist leiser als der Fernseher
translation_en: The radio is quieter than the TV
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'leiser'?
explanation_en: Comparatives are built by adding '-er' to the adjective, just like English 'quiet → quieter'. Then follows 'als', which means 'than'. So the pattern is simply: adjective + er + als
explanation_tr: Karşılaştırma derecesi sıfata '-er' eklenerek yapılır. Karşılaştırmayı tamamlayan kelime 'als'tır. Kalıp şudur: sıfat + er + als

sentence_de: Ich muss meine E-Mails checken
translation_en: I have to check my emails
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'muss'?
explanation_en: Modal verbs like 'müssen' always push the main verb to the end in its infinitive form. 'muss' expresses obligation. The main verb 'checken' sits at the very end unchanged
explanation_tr: müssen' gibi modal fiiller ana fiili her zaman mastar hâlinde cümlenin sonuna iter. Ana fiil 'checken' yalın hâlde en sona gider

sentence_de: Ich höre Radio, weil ich Musik mag
translation_en: I listen to the radio because I like music
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'weil'?
explanation_en: weil' flips the normal word order, the verb jumps to the end. Think of it as saving the most important word for last
explanation_tr: weil' normal kelime sırasını tersine çevirir, fiil yan cümlenin sonuna gider. Bu, en önemli kelimeyi en sona saklamak gibidir

sentence_de: Ich freue mich auf das neue Video
translation_en: I am looking forward to the new video
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'mich'?
explanation_en: sich freuen auf' is a fixed reflexive expression. The reflexive pronoun always matches the subject. For 'ich' that pronoun is 'mich'
explanation_tr: sich freuen auf' dönüşlü (Reflexive) ifadedir. Dönüşlü zamir her zaman özneye göre çekimlenir. 'ich' için bu 'mich'tir

sentence_de: Ich antworte so schnell wie möglich
translation_en: I reply as fast as possible
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'so...wie'?
explanation_en: There are two comparison patterns; 'als' for unequal things and 'so...wie' for equal things. Think of it as 'as...as' in English. The adjective between them never changes its form
explanation_tr: İki karşılaştırma kalıbı vardır; eşit olmayan şeyler için 'als', eşit şeyler için 'so...wie'. İkisi arasındaki sıfat yalın hâldedir

sentence_de: Er schreibt mir jeden Tag eine Nachricht
translation_en: He writes me a message every day
translation_tr: Her gün bana bir mesaj yazıyor
question_en: Which of the following is correct for 'mir'?
explanation_en: Two objects; 'eine Nachricht' is what he writes (accusative) and 'mir' is who receives it (dative). The receiver is always dative
